gate_type: no gate on cron callback — WP-Cron fires without authentication; exploitation blocked by HTTPS channel to realfavicongenerator.net (requires MitM or server compromise)
reasoning: The cron callback check_for_updates() registered via add_action(ACTION_CHECK_FOR_UPDATE, ...) at admin.php line 43 has zero authentication, nonce, or capability checks of its own. is_admin() in the main plugin file (line 47) is not a real gate because WordPress sets is_admin()=true during WP-Cron execution, so the admin class loads and the callback fires normally on any HTTP request that triggers cron. There is also no DOING_CRON guard on the callback. However, the actual attack path — injecting a malicious ZIP — requires either (a) a MitM of the HTTPS connection to realfavicongenerator.net or (b) compromise of the realfavicongenerator.net server itself. These are infrastructure-level prerequisites that a standard unauthenticated WordPress attacker cannot achieve. No nonce, secret key, random token, or capability check protects the cron callback, but the upstream HTTPS channel acts as the effective barrier. Because exploitation depends on breaking TLS or compromising an external server (not a WordPress-level gate), gate_breachable is false and severity is downgraded to LOW for default-install risk assessment. The finding remains valid as an architectural weakness (no signature verification on downloaded updates), but is not default-exploitable in a standard deployment.
